channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: QuiX Quantum Joins QuantumBW and Photonics BW Networks
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights the growing importance of regional quantum ecosystems as companies recognize the value of localized collaboration networks. QuiX Quantum's decision to join these Baden-Württemberg networks reflects a broader trend where quantum hardware companies are strategically positioning themselves within European innovation hubs to accelerate technology transfer and forge industry partnerships. The move is particularly significant given Baden-Württemberg's emergence as a quantum powerhouse, home to major research institutions and a thriving photonics industry that's crucial for photonic quantum computing platforms like those QuiX develops. This type of regional clustering could prove essential for bridging the gap between quantum research and commercial applications across Europe.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QuiX Quantum is pleased to announce its membership in QuantumBW and Photonics BW, two leading Baden-Württemberg networks driving collaboration across quantum technologies, photonics, research, industry and technology transfer. As a Dutch-German deep-tech company, QuiX Quantum is already actively engaged in the Baden-Württemberg regional innovation ecosystem. The company has established an […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: QNu Labs Takes India’s Quantum Cybersecurity to Bharat Innovates 2026, Signs Research Deal with Eindhoven University of Technology
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights India's growing ambitions in quantum cybersecurity as QNu Labs showcases their quantum key distribution technology at a major international innovation summit. The company's new research partnership with Eindhoven University of Technology demonstrates how Indian quantum startups are building crucial academic bridges to advance their technologies on the global stage. QNu Labs' presence at Bharat Innovates 2026 reflects India's strategic push to position itself as a quantum technology leader, particularly in the critical area of quantum-safe communications where early commercial applications are already emerging. This kind of international collaboration and visibility is essential for quantum startups to scale their technologies and compete in the rapidly evolving global quantum market.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — QNu Labs is in Nice, France this week at Bharat Innovates 2026 a global platform organised by India’s Ministry of Education and the French Ministry to connect deep tech founders with global enterprises, investors, research institutions, and policymakers to showcase their breakthrough innovation to the world. The company is one […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Strong coupling of a microwave photon to an electron on helium
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough demonstrates strong coupling between a single electron floating on superfluid helium and a microwave cavity photon, marking a significant advance for this exotic qubit platform. Electrons on helium have long been theoretically attractive as qubits due to their exceptional isolation from environmental noise, but practical readout has remained challenging. The achievement of strong coupling now opens the door to efficient single-shot qubit readout, bringing helium-based quantum processors closer to practical implementation. This work adds another promising platform to the growing ecosystem of quantum computing architectures, potentially offering unique advantages in coherence times and scalability.

Nature Physics, Published online: 15 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03342-z Individual electrons on superfluid helium can be used as qubits. Strong coupling between the motional state of a single superfluid helium-bound electron and a single cavity photon has now been demonstrated, enabling qubit readout schemes.

Read original at Nature Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IBM is Using AI to Help Identify New Quantum Error Correction Codes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a fascinating convergence where IBM is applying artificial intelligence to tackle one of quantum computing's most fundamental challenges: finding better error correction codes. The traditional approach of manually searching through the vast mathematical space of potential quantum error correction schemes is prohibitively slow, making IBM's OpenEvolve framework a potentially game-changing tool that could accelerate the discovery of more efficient codes. This AI-driven approach represents a smart meta-strategy where classical machine learning helps optimize the very foundation that will enable fault-tolerant quantum computers to function reliably. The open-source nature of OpenEvolve also means the broader quantum research community can contribute to and benefit from these advances, potentially speeding up the timeline toward practical, error-corrected quantum systems.

Logical Error Rate Performance for Given Physical Error Rates for Different QEC Codes. Credit:IBM Searching for optimal Quantum Error Correction (QEC) codes is an incredibly time-consuming and computationally demanding bottleneck due to the vast space of potential algebraic formulations. To address this, IBM researchers have introduced OpenEvolve, an open-source, LLM-guided evolutionary AI framework that dramatically [...] The post IBM is Using AI to Help Identify New Quantum Error Correction Codes appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Microsoft And Quantinuum Report on Major Gains in Quantum Error Correction
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration between Microsoft and Quantinuum represents a crucial milestone in quantum computing's journey toward practical applications, as error correction has been the field's most formidable challenge for decades. The ability to actually demonstrate major gains in quantum error correction moves the technology significantly closer to achieving fault-tolerant quantum computers that can run complex algorithms reliably. What makes this particularly noteworthy is that it validates theoretical approaches to error correction in real hardware, showing that the quantum computing industry is transitioning from proof-of-concept demonstrations to engineering solutions that could enable commercially viable quantum systems. This breakthrough could accelerate the timeline for quantum computers to tackle real-world problems in drug discovery, cryptography, and optimization that have been waiting for error rates to drop below critical thresholds.

Insider Brief Quantum computers have long promised to solve problems beyond the reach of conventional machines. Yet the field has faced a persistent obstacle. The quantum bits, or qubits, that store and process information are extremely fragile. Even minor disturbances from their environment can introduce errors that accumulate and overwhelm a calculation before it finishes. […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: What Quantum Can Learn from the Data Center Playbook
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges: how to transition from experimental lab systems to industrial-scale infrastructure that can support real applications. The comparison to data center evolution is particularly insightful, as it highlights how successful computing paradigms require standardized hardware, robust software stacks, and scalable operational frameworks - areas where quantum computing is still finding its footing. As quantum systems move beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations, lessons from decades of data center industrialization could prove invaluable for building the quantum cloud services and hybrid computing platforms that will define the field's commercial future. This perspective shift from pure technological development to infrastructure thinking represents a crucial maturation step for the quantum industry.

Guest Post by Komala S Madineedi Every new compute paradigm eventually gets measured against the data center playbook. Not because data centers represent the endpoint of computing, but because they represent one of the most successful examples of compute industrialization. Over decades, computing evolved from individual hardware components into layered systems of infrastructure, platforms, ecosystems, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Threat to Crypto Is Not Here Yet, but Coinbase Advisory Board Says the Time to Prepare Is Now
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Coinbase-commissioned report marks a significant shift in the cryptocurrency industry's approach to quantum threats, moving from speculative timelines to practical preparation strategies. While cryptographically relevant quantum computers remain years away, the report wisely emphasizes that transitioning billions of dollars in cryptocurrency infrastructure to quantum-resistant algorithms will require extensive planning and gradual implementation. The timing is particularly crucial since blockchain systems, once deployed, are notoriously difficult to upgrade due to their decentralized nature and the need for consensus among participants. This proactive stance from a major crypto exchange signals that the industry is finally taking seriously the long-term challenge of maintaining security in a post-quantum world.

Insider Brief The cryptocurrency industry should stop debating exactly when quantum computers will become a threat and start preparing for their eventual arrival, according to a new report commissioned by Coinbase and prepared by a group of leading experts in quantum computing, cryptography and blockchain technology. The report concludes that large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum computers capable […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SEALSQ and WISeKey Outline Strategic Progress on Space-Based Quantum Spatial Orbital Cloud
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights an ambitious intersection of quantum cryptography and space technology, as SEALSQ and WISeKey advance their plans for deploying post-quantum security infrastructure directly in orbit. The Quantum Spatial Orbital Cloud concept represents a fascinating approach to addressing quantum-safe communications by placing tamper-resistant cryptographic nodes beyond terrestrial interference, potentially offering unprecedented security for global communications networks. While space-based quantum infrastructure remains in early development stages, this initiative reflects growing recognition that the quantum threat to current encryption requires innovative deployment strategies that extend beyond traditional ground-based solutions. The combination of certified quantum randomness generation and post-quantum cryptography in LEO satellites could eventually provide a robust foundation for securing critical communications against both classical and quantum computational attacks.

Post-quantum semiconductor developer SEALSQ Corp and its parent company WISeKey International Holding Ltd have detailed engineering and deployment milestones for their Quantum Spatial Orbital Cloud (QSOC) platform. The space-based infrastructure architecture is designed to configure low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellites as independent, tamper-resistant computing nodes delivering post-quantum cryptography (PQC), certified quantum randomness, and localized edge processing [...] The post SEALSQ and WISeKey Outline Strategic Progress on Space-Based Quantum Spatial Orbital Cloud appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IBM Quantum Releases Open-Source “ffsim” Library for Specialized Fermionic Circuit Simulation
item.description:
Curator's Take: This open-source release from IBM addresses a critical bottleneck in quantum chemistry and materials science research, where classical simulation of fermionic systems becomes exponentially challenging as system size grows. The ffsim library's specialized focus on fermion simulation could accelerate development of quantum algorithms for drug discovery, catalyst design, and understanding complex materials by providing researchers with faster benchmarking tools to validate their quantum approaches before running on actual quantum hardware. By making this capability freely available to the quantum community, IBM is fostering collaborative development in one of quantum computing's most promising near-term applications, where even modest quantum advantages could yield significant scientific breakthroughs. The timing is particularly strategic as quantum chemistry applications are among the leading candidates for demonstrating practical quantum advantage in the coming years.

IBM Quantum researchers have introduced ffsim, an open-source Python library engineered for the high-performance classical simulation of quantum circuits that model fermions—the fundamental particles constituting atoms, molecules, and condensed matter. Detailed in a technical preprint deposited on the arXiv repository, the library was developed to supply the quantum information community with faster validation and benchmarking [...] The post IBM Quantum Releases Open-Source “ffsim” Library for Specialized Fermionic Circuit Simulation appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Microsoft and Quantinuum Publish Peer-Reviewed Quantum Error Correction Data in Nature
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Nature publication marks a significant milestone in quantum computing's progression toward practical fault-tolerant systems, as Microsoft and Quantinuum have demonstrated measurable improvements in logical qubit error rates through their collaborative approach combining software virtualization with trapped-ion hardware. The peer-reviewed validation of their quantum error correction implementations provides crucial experimental evidence that we're moving beyond theoretical QEC schemes toward real-world quantum fault tolerance. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the partnership model itself - Microsoft's software expertise in qubit virtualization paired with Quantinuum's advanced trapped-ion architecture represents the kind of collaborative approach that may accelerate the timeline for commercially viable quantum computers. The publication in Nature, one of science's most prestigious journals, underscores the rigor and significance of these experimental results for the broader quantum computing community.

Microsoft and trapped-ion hardware developer Quantinuum have published a peer-reviewed paper in the journal Nature detailing physical implementations of quantum error correction (QEC). The paper, titled “Improved quantum processor logical error rates via correction and detection,” presents experimental data collected using Microsoft’s qubit-virtualization platform executed on Quantinuum’s trapped-ion Quantum Charge-Coupled Device (QCCD) hardware architecture. The [...] The post Microsoft and Quantinuum Publish Peer-Reviewed Quantum Error Correction Data in Nature appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum geometrical description of hole spin qubits far away from the $Γ$-point
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental challenge in hole spin qubits by developing a more complete theoretical framework that accounts for spin-orbit interactions far from the standard reference point in momentum space. The key finding that different types of spin-orbit coupling cannot be simultaneously turned off reveals an important limitation for achieving the "perfect spin-orbit switch" that researchers have been pursuing for optimal qubit control. By deriving non-perturbative effective Hamiltonians for various germanium-based quantum dot systems, this work provides theorists and experimentalists with more accurate tools to predict and optimize hole spin qubit behavior across different material platforms. This improved theoretical foundation is particularly valuable as the field pushes toward practical quantum processors where precise control over qubit dynamics becomes increasingly critical.

Hole spin qubits provide one of the leading platforms for spin-based quantum computing due to their large intrinsic spin-orbit interaction (SOI), which enables fast electrical manipulation. The SOI of planar quantum dots has mostly been investigated in theoretical studies by examining the SOI already present in the two-dimensional hole gas (2DHG). Here, we study the SOI created by the in-plane confinement by deriving non-perturbative effective Hamiltonians numerically for hole spin qubits. We find that the quantum geometry of the 2DHG naturally emerges, leading to a meaningful non-perturbative definition of pseudospin valid far away from the $Γ$-point. The SOI of the 2DHG and of the in-plane confinement have different forms; therefore, they cannot be turned off simultaneously, ruining the perfect spin-orbit switch functionality of spin qubits. We construct effective Hamiltonians using the symmetry approach for various low-dimensional hole systems: (i) a heavy-hole confined in a SiGe/Ge/SiGe heterostructure, (ii) a light-hole confined in SnGe/Ge, (iii) a gate-defined nanowire in SiGe/Ge/SiGe, and (iv) a hole confined in a Ge/Si core/shell nanowire. The non-perturbative effective Hamiltonians provide results with excellent agreement with the full Hamiltonians.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: SEALSQ Advances Satellite-Based Post-Quantum Security Platform
item.description:
Curator's Take: SEALSQ's Quantum Spatial Orbital Cloud represents a fascinating convergence of post-quantum cryptography and space-based infrastructure, addressing the urgent need for quantum-resistant security systems before large-scale quantum computers threaten current encryption standards. This satellite-based approach is particularly intriguing because it could provide quantum-secure communications to regions with limited terrestrial infrastructure while potentially offering enhanced security through the physical isolation of space-based systems. The timing is strategic, as organizations worldwide are racing to implement post-quantum cryptographic solutions ahead of the anticipated "Y2Q" moment when quantum computers will break today's encryption methods. While still in development, this orbital security platform illustrates how the post-quantum transition is driving innovation across multiple technology domains beyond just algorithm development.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — SEALSQ Corp (NASDAQ: LAES), a leader in post-quantum semiconductors, cybersecurity and digital identity technologies, today announced significant progress in the development of the Quantum Spatial Orbital Cloud (QSOC), a next-generation orbital platform designed to deliver post-quantum-secure services directly from space. The initiative represents a new category of digital infrastructure where […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum gates with parametrically driven multi-qubit couplers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most pressing challenges in scaling up quantum computers: how to efficiently connect qubits that aren't physically adjacent to each other. The proposed four-qubit plaquette design with a central tunable coupler is particularly clever because it enables high-fidelity gates between diagonal qubits without requiring additional physical connections, potentially reducing the routing overhead that plagues current quantum processors. The demonstrated 99.9% fidelity for 50-nanosecond gates, even when running multiple gates in parallel, suggests this architecture could significantly improve both the connectivity and operational efficiency of superconducting quantum devices. Most intriguingly, the ability to perform native three-qubit gates opens up new possibilities for implementing quantum algorithms more directly, rather than decomposing everything into two-qubit operations.

Superconducting quantum processors could significantly profit from enhanced connectivity together with precise control of interactions and gates between qubits. Here we investigate plaquettes of four qubits that are coupled via a central tunable coupling circuit, so that not only gates between qubits connected by an edge of the plaquette can be executed but also between qubits across the diagonal. By numerically and analytically analyzing parametrically driven processes, we explore $\sqrt{\text{iSWAP}}$-gates between any pair of qubits, also across the diagonal, as well as three-qubit interactions and gates. For experimentally available circuit parameters, we for example find $\sqrt{\text{iSWAP}}$-gates with a gate time of 50 ns and 99.9\% fidelity, which is decreased to 99.4\% if two such gates are executed in parallel on disjoint qubit pairs in the plaquette. For three-qubit gates we find fidelities of 95\% fidelity at a gate time of 200 ns.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Physics-Informed Variational Quantum Classifier for Phase Detection in Strongly Correlated Matter
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a compelling fusion of quantum machine learning and many-body physics, using a variational quantum classifier that's built directly from the physics of the problem rather than generic ansatzes. What makes this particularly exciting is that the classifier automatically discovers optimal experimental protocols for distinguishing quantum phases, essentially learning how to design better quantum sensing experiments. The successful demonstration on IBM's superconducting hardware shows this isn't just theoretical - despite noise and decoherence, the system maintained its ability to classify different topological phases. This physics-informed approach could represent a new paradigm for quantum algorithms, where the quantum computer doesn't just solve problems but actively learns the best way to probe quantum matter.

The characterisation of quantum phases in strongly correlated systems is a crucial milestone for the deployment of quantum sensors. In this work, we present a Physics-Informed Variational Quantum Classifier (VQC) designed to detect the topological phase transition between the Fermi polaron quasiparticle and the molecular bound state. Unlike conventional Machine Learning approaches, our quantum architecture is constructed via the Trotterised time-evolution of an effective Hamiltonian, ensuring that the learnable parameters correspond to interpretable physical quantities. We show that the VQC efficiently discovers the optimal interferometric protocol, specifically the evolution time and effective bath interactions required to maximise the visibility of Ramsey fringes, thereby clearly distinguishing the Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) and Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) regimes. Furthermore, we report the validation of this classifier on the QRed superconducting quantum processor (BSC-CNS). Despite the intrinsic hardware noise and decoherence, the VQC preserves the relative ordering of the topological phases. We demonstrate that the physics-informed architecture achieves a linear gate complexity $\mathcal{O}(N)$, bypassing the exponential memory wall of classical simulation and ensuring scalability to many-body regimes.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Horizon: An evaluation of quantum computing as a threat to Bitcoin and Ethereum
item.description:
Curator's Take: This comprehensive analysis cuts through the quantum-crypto hype by providing the first rigorous, data-driven assessment of when quantum computers might actually threaten major cryptocurrencies. The researchers make crucial distinctions that are often missed in public discourse: while Shor's algorithm could eventually break the cryptographic signatures that authorize Bitcoin and Ethereum transactions, Grover's algorithm poses minimal threat to proof-of-work mining due to fundamental limitations in quantum speedup and parallelization. Perhaps most importantly, the study reveals that the threat is largely mitigable through existing post-quantum cryptography solutions, with the real challenge being governance and coordination rather than technical feasibility. With their Monte Carlo modeling suggesting meaningful quantum threat probabilities of 17% by 2035 and 60% by 2050, this work provides blockchain developers and policymakers with the quantitative framework needed to plan migration strategies before it's too late.

Quantum computing poses a real, broad-based, but bounded and substantially mitigable threat to Bitcoin and Ethereum. We separate the two quantum algorithms that public discussion routinely conflates: Shor's algorithm breaks the elliptic-curve signatures (ECDSA over secp256k1, BLS over BLS12-381) that authorize spending, whereas Grover's algorithm does not meaningfully threaten proof-of-work mining, which is protected by a merely quadratic speedup, fault-tolerant per-operation costs, a square-root parallelization wall, and difficulty adjustment. Folding hardware scaling, the falling resource requirement, a fault-tolerance readiness lag, and expert surveys into a single Monte-Carlo forecast yields a wide, bimodal arrival distribution for a cryptographically relevant quantum computer: about a one-in-six chance by 2035, near 30% by 2040, and about 60% by 2050. Exposure is concentrated and mostly migratable: of Bitcoin's roughly six million quantum-exposed coins only about 2.3 million are irreducibly at risk, while 50 to 65% of Ether sits at key-revealed accounts that can adopt post-quantum signatures. A timely migration beats even an optimistic 2035 machine, so the binding constraint is governance, not technology. A survey of the top twenty cryptocurrencies finds none fully post-quantum. Reproducible models accompany every quantitative claim.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Sensitivity of polaron-molecule observables to MDR/GUP-like ultraviolet deformations at low energies via quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a fascinating intersection of quantum gravity theory and practical quantum computing, using IBM's quantum hardware to explore how tiny modifications to fundamental physics might be detectable in many-body quantum systems. The researchers cleverly exploit the polaron-molecule crossover region where small ultraviolet deformations get amplified, potentially offering a new experimental window into quantum gravity effects that are normally completely inaccessible at low energies. What makes this particularly exciting is the demonstration on actual quantum hardware (the QRed superconducting processor), showing that near-term quantum computers might serve as sensitive probes for fundamental physics beyond the Standard Model. This work opens up the intriguing possibility that quantum simulators could become tools for testing theories of quantum gravity, bridging the gap between high-energy physics and accessible laboratory experiments.

We show that impurity many-body observables can display enhanced sensitivity to ultraviolet deformations of generalized-uncertainty-principle and modified-dispersion-relation type at accessible energy scales. Using a deformed polaron-molecule Hamiltonian constructed to preserve the infrared sector, we quantify the impact of such deformations on spectral and Ramsey observables and implement the corresponding dynamics in a controlled quantum computing setting. We identify regimes near the polaron-molecule crossover where small ultraviolet deformations are strongly amplified, leading to experimentally resolvable changes in quasiparticle properties and spectral response. Our results establish a concrete sensitivity-based route to low-energy quantum-gravity phenomenology in a well-defined many-body platform and delimit the validity of the effective description. Furthermore, we report experimental validation on the QRed superconducting quantum processor (BSC-CNS).

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: HKU Engineering Develops ‘Brain-Like’ Chip to Advance Quantum Computing And Deep-Space Exploration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research represents an intriguing convergence of neuromorphic computing and quantum technologies, addressing one of the most pressing challenges in quantum systems: the need for sophisticated control electronics that can operate in the extreme cold required by quantum processors. HKU's cryogenic neuromorphic chip could potentially solve the "wiring problem" that plagues current quantum computers, where thousands of classical control lines must snake from room temperature into dilution refrigerators, creating heat loads and limiting scalability. By placing brain-inspired processing directly at cryogenic temperatures, this approach could enable more efficient qubit control while reducing the classical-quantum interface bottleneck that currently constrains system performance. The dual application to deep-space exploration also highlights how quantum computing innovations often drive advances in other extreme-environment technologies.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Researchers from Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering under the Faculty of Engineering at the University of Hong Kong (HKU) and the Centre for Advanced Semiconductors and Integrated Circuits (CASIC), have achieved a major breakthrough in cryogenic electronics. The team have developed a programmable neuromorphic hardware platform that operates near […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum sensing through bosonic-fermionic Bell-state transitions in two-photon interference
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research introduces a clever twist on the widely-used Hong-Ou-Mandel interference technique by manipulating the quantum exchange symmetry of photon pairs rather than relying on traditional path-based measurements. The breakthrough lies in creating a sensing method that's immune to the optical losses and alignment issues that plague conventional HOM sensors, while maintaining exceptional sensitivity by continuously transitioning between bosonic and fermionic-like Bell states. By achieving birefringence measurements with 10^-6 resolution across broad temperature ranges, this approach opens new possibilities for robust quantum sensors that could operate reliably in real-world conditions where traditional interferometric methods struggle. The work represents a significant step toward making quantum sensing more practical by exploiting fundamental quantum symmetries rather than fragile optical configurations.

Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference has become a central resource for quantum sensing and metrology owing to its sensitivity to temporal delay and photon indistinguishability. However, existing HOM-based sensing schemes generally rely on inserting a sample into one arm of the interferometer, making the measurement vulnerable to optical loss, alignment instability, and bandwidth-dependent distortion of the interference profile. Here, we demonstrate a symmetry-controlled quantum sensing scheme based on continuous transitions between symmetric (bosonic-like) and antisymmetric (fermionic-like) Bell states in two-photon interference. By imprinting a geometric phase onto the classical pump beam and transferring it to polarization-entangled photons generated via spontaneous parametric down-conversion, we coherently tune the exchange symmetry of the entangled state without altering the temporal or spectral indistinguishability of the photons. The HOM response evolves continuously from bunching to antibunching with a sine square phase dependence, producing a coincidence modulation of approximately 10 * 10^4 counts s^-1 counts/s. In contrast to conventional HOM sensing, the phase-modulation linewidth remains fixed at pi/2, independent of photon bandwidth. Using a birefringent crystal placed directly in the pump beam, we measure thermo-dispersive birefringence with a resolution of the order of 10^{-6} over a broad temperature range. Our results establish exchange symmetry as a controllable resource for robust quantum sensing and symmetry-engineered photonic quantum information processing.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: ORCA Computing Joins Digital Realty Innovation Lab in London
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership represents a crucial step toward making quantum computing more commercially viable by addressing one of the field's biggest practical challenges: integrating quantum systems into existing data center infrastructure. ORCA Computing's photonic quantum computers are particularly well-suited for this type of testing environment since they can operate at room temperature, unlike many superconducting quantum systems that require elaborate cooling setups. The collaboration with Digital Realty, a major data center operator, signals that the industry is moving beyond laboratory demonstrations toward real-world deployment scenarios where quantum and classical systems must work seamlessly together. This kind of infrastructure partnership could accelerate the timeline for quantum computing applications by providing a controlled environment to work out integration challenges before full commercial rollouts.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — ORCA Computing announced its participation in the newly launched Digital Realty Innovation Lab (DRIL) in London, a next-generation infrastructure testing environment developed by Digital Realty, the world’s largest cloud-and carrier-neutral data center provider. The DRIL enables customers to test emerging AI and quantum technologies in live operational conditions before full-scale deployment, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Google Declined U.S. Quantum Funding Initiative Over Program Restrictions
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a fascinating tension between government quantum funding ambitions and industry innovation timelines that could significantly impact U.S. quantum competitiveness. Google's decision to forgo $2 billion in federal support suggests that bureaucratic constraints and program requirements may be hampering rather than helping America's quantum development efforts, particularly concerning given China's aggressive state-led quantum investments. The move raises important questions about whether current U.S. quantum policy frameworks are optimally designed to leverage private sector capabilities, especially when companies like Google are already making substantial progress on quantum hardware and error correction. This development could signal a need for more flexible public-private partnerships that balance oversight requirements with the rapid iteration cycles essential for quantum breakthroughs.

Insider Brief Google chose not to participate in the U.S. government’s recent $2 billion quantum technology funding initiative because the conditions attached to the program would have slowed the company’s development efforts, according to comments made by a senior executive this week. Speaking at the Semafor Tech Summit in San Francisco on June 10, Google […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Trapped-Ion Quantum Computing Companies in 2026
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article provides valuable perspective on trapped-ion quantum computing's strategic position as the field enters a critical scaling phase in 2026. While trapped-ion systems may lag behind superconducting qubits in raw numbers and photonic approaches in operational costs, their superior gate fidelity represents a crucial advantage for near-term quantum applications where accuracy trumps scale. The piece likely explores how companies like IonQ, Alpine Quantum Technologies, and others are leveraging this precision advantage to target specific commercial niches, particularly as the industry moves beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations toward practical quantum advantage. This focus on quality over quantity could prove decisive as businesses seek reliable quantum solutions for optimization, chemistry simulation, and cryptography applications.

Insider Brief Of the major quantum computing approaches in active commercial development, trapped-ion systems hold a specific and well-established position. They are not the fastest to scale in terms of raw qubit counts, and they are not the cheapest to operate. What they offer is gate fidelity – the accuracy with which quantum operations execute […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Arkeon Lands Seed Investment to Boost Quantum Chip Production Yields
item.description:
Curator's Take: This seed funding for Arkeon highlights a critical but often overlooked challenge in quantum computing: manufacturing consistency. While much attention focuses on qubit counts and error rates, the reality is that superconducting quantum chips suffer from significant fabrication variations that can make identical designs perform vastly differently. Arkeon's approach to improving production yields addresses a fundamental bottleneck that could accelerate the path to commercially viable quantum computers by making high-quality chips more reliably manufacturable. The backing from Chalmers Ventures, connected to one of Europe's leading quantum research institutions, suggests this Swedish startup may have identified genuine solutions to problems that have plagued the industry since its early days.

Insider Brief Swedish startup Arkeon has raised €594,200 (SEK 6.5 million) in seed funding to advance a manufacturing technology designed to improve the consistency and performance of superconducting quantum chips, according to information reported by EU Startups. The investment was led by Chalmers Ventures, with participation from Navigare Ventures and Almi Invest. The company said […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Real-time pseudo entropy and modular-Hamiltonian correlations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article introduces a fascinating new theoretical framework that connects quantum entanglement dynamics to the fundamental structure of quantum information processing. By revealing how "pseudo entropy" - a complex-valued extension of entanglement entropy - responds to real-time evolution through correlations between system Hamiltonians and modular Hamiltonians, the researchers provide deep insights into how quantum subsystems exchange information during unitary evolution. The work demonstrates that the imaginary component of this pseudo entropy isn't just mathematical bookkeeping but represents genuine physical information about how microscopic time evolution interacts with coarse-grained subsystem descriptions. This theoretical advance could prove valuable for understanding decoherence, designing quantum error correction protocols, and optimizing quantum algorithms that rely on entanglement structure.

Pseudo entropy is a complex-valued generalization of entanglement entropy defined from a reduced transition matrix. We study the pseudo entropy associated with a real-time transition matrix between an initial pure state and its unitary time evolution. For a subsystem $A$, we show that the short-time behavior of real-time pseudo entropy is governed by the correlation between the physical Hamiltonian $H$ and the modular Hamiltonian $K_A=-\logρ_A$ of the initial reduced state, $ S_A(t,0)=S_A(0)-it \langle K_A(H-\langle H\rangle)\rangle + \mathcal{O}(t^2)$. For Hermitian dynamics, the initial imaginary response is controlled by the symmetrized covariance of $H$ and $K_A$ with an overall minus sign, while the initial real response is governed by their commutator. Thus the imaginary part of real-time pseudo entropy is not merely a branch artifact: it is a time-oriented modular response generated by the correlation between microscopic time evolution and subsystem coarse graining. We clarify the relation of this result to the known first law of pseudo entropy, derive an all-order expression in a Schmidt-diagonal model, recover thermal pseudo entropy as a special case, illustrate the covariance/commutator decomposition in a two-qubit model, and confirm the covariance response in transverse-field Ising-chain quenches, including a finite-size study of a modular susceptibility near the Ising critical region. We discuss how this amplitude-level oriented response can be related to ordinary entropy production, and also give a concrete $\mathcal{PT}$-symmetric toy-model illustration of the non-Hermitian extension.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Fourier analysis of quantum neural network with non-linear data embedding
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research significantly advances our theoretical understanding of quantum neural networks by extending Fourier analysis beyond the commonly studied angle embedding to amplitude embedding schemes, which offer more compact data representations. The key finding that noise actually suppresses the variance of Fourier coefficients by an exponential factor provides crucial insights for both the expressivity limitations and the notorious barren plateau problem that plagues quantum machine learning. By rigorously proving that most Fourier coefficients concentrate near zero in noisy environments, this work helps explain why quantum neural networks struggle to learn complex patterns and offers a mathematical foundation for developing more robust quantum ML algorithms. The extension to non-integer frequencies and practical validation through simulations makes this theoretical framework immediately relevant for practitioners working on real quantum machine learning applications.

Fourier analysis has become a crucial tool for understanding the expressivity of Variational Quantum Circuit (VQC) models, as well as an important indicator of barren plateaus (BP). While existing literature has only studied angle-embedded VQCs in a noiseless environment, here we develop the Fourier analysis of VQCs with non-linear data embedding, with particular focus on amplitude embedding, which provides a naturally compact encoding scheme. We first investigate a subtle difference in the domain of input features within amplitude embedding that leads to a distinct expressivity of the zero-frequency Fourier coefficient. By assuming that the ensemble of unitaries generated from the parameter space forms at least a 2-design with respect to the unitary group, we derive, via Weingarten calculus, that the mean of the Fourier coefficients is concentrated at zero, and the variance scales at an exponentially decaying order with respect to the multi-dimensional frequency magnitude. When a noise channel with unitary Kraus operators and probabilities $\{p_k\}$ is taken into account, the variance is further suppressed by a factor $\left(\sum_k p_k^2\right)^{Q}<1$, where $Q$ is the number of channel instances applied. Furthermore, we demonstrate and validate the analytical results through simulations, both noiseless and noisy, including a case where target functions are decomposed into non-integer frequencies, highlighting the practical utility of the approach. Our results establish a rigorous Fourier framework for amplitude-encoded VQCs, offering both theoretical guarantees on expressivity, hence trainability scaling in the frequency domain, as well as practical simulations for deployment on noisy quantum devices.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Simultaneous Estimation of Partial-Transpose Moments with Active Memory Independent of the Moment Order
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles a fundamental challenge in quantum state characterization by developing memory-efficient methods to simultaneously estimate multiple partial-transpose moments, which are crucial for detecting quantum entanglement and understanding bipartite quantum systems. The breakthrough lies in achieving constant memory usage (just 2n+1 qubits) regardless of how many moments are being estimated, while maintaining near-optimal sample complexity that scales only logarithmically worse than theoretical limits. This work extends beyond theoretical interest because partial-transpose moments are essential tools for entanglement detection and quantum state tomography, and the memory-efficient approach could make such characterizations practical on near-term quantum devices with limited qubit resources. The authors' demonstration that you can extract rich spectral information about quantum states without exponentially scaling memory requirements represents a significant step toward scalable quantum state analysis protocols.

We study the simultaneous estimation of partial-transpose moments $p_j(ρ_{AB})=\mathrm{Tr}[(ρ_{AB}^{T_B})^j]$, $j=2,\ldots,K$, of an unknown bipartite $n$-qubit state from independent copies under an explicit active-memory constraint. We give a sequential qubit-reuse realization of the partial-transpose permutation that uses at most $2n+1$ active qubits, independent of $K$, and estimates all moments $p_2,\ldots,p_K$ to uniform additive error $ε$ with total copy complexity $O(K\log K/ε^2)$. We also prove two converse bounds. First, any uniformly accurate simultaneous estimator requires $Ω(K/ε^2)$ copies in the worst case. Second, the same scaling holds on an explicit isospectral two-qubit negative-partial-transpose (NPT) family whose ordinary moments are constant while the partial-transpose moments vary. These results characterize the copy complexity of the partial-transpose moment hierarchy up to a logarithmic factor and extend simultaneous nonlinear-functional estimation from ordinary state powers to partial-transpose spectral data under active quantum memory independent of the target moment order.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Alumni Ventures Backs Yaqumo in First Japan Investment
item.description:
Curator's Take: This investment marks a significant milestone for Japan's quantum computing ecosystem, as Yaqumo becomes the first Japanese quantum startup to attract funding from Alumni Ventures, signaling growing international confidence in Japan's quantum capabilities. Yaqumo's focus on neutral-atom quantum computing puts them in competition with global players like QuEra and Pasqal, representing an important technology approach that offers advantages in scalability and qubit connectivity. The cross-border investment highlights how the quantum computing landscape is becoming increasingly global, with venture capital flowing across continents to support promising quantum technologies regardless of geographic boundaries. This funding could help Japan strengthen its position in the international quantum race, particularly as the country works to build a comprehensive quantum industry beyond just research institutions.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Yaqumo Inc. (“Yaqumo”), a startup developing neutral-atom quantum computers in Japan, today announced that it has raised funding in a seed extension round from Alumni Ventures, LLC (“Alumni Ventures”), a venture capital firm headquartered in Manchester, New Hampshire, United States. This marks Alumni Ventures’ first investment in Japan. The investment was […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Note on the local calculation of decoherence of quantum superposition in the static black holes
item.description:
Curator's Take: This theoretical work provides crucial insights into how quantum superpositions would behave near black holes, directly addressing one of the most fundamental challenges in quantum computing: environmental decoherence. The research reveals that the choice of quantum vacuum state around a black hole dramatically affects decoherence rates, with Hawking radiation creating thermal noise that destroys quantum coherence while certain vacuum states preserve it. This has profound implications for understanding the fundamental limits of quantum information processing in curved spacetime and could inform future quantum technologies that must operate in extreme gravitational environments. The mathematical framework developed here also advances our theoretical toolkit for analyzing decoherence in complex quantum field environments, which remains one of the biggest obstacles to scaling quantum computers.

We investigate the decoherence of a quantum spatial superposition of a static particle in Schwarzschild and Reissner-Nordström black holes. By treating the particle as a localized classical source coupled to a quantum scalar field, we reformulate the decoherence process in the Danielson-Satishchandran-Wald (DSW) gedankenexperiment through coherent state generation and derive the local expression for the decoherence functional in terms of the Wightman function. In the long-time limit, the decoherence rate is shown to be characterized by the low-frequency behavior of the Wightman function. We then employ the asymptotic matching method to calculate the analytical expressions of the Wightman functions in the Boulware, Unruh, and Hartle-Hawking vacua. We show that the decoherence behavior depends on the quantum state of the environmental field. While the Boulware vacuum gives vanishing decoherence for a static superposition, the thermal effects associated with Hawking radiation in the Unruh and Hartle-Hawking vacua can induce nonvanishing decoherence.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Entanglement of Bethe States
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles a fundamental question in quantum many-body physics by systematically mapping how quantum entanglement varies across the complete spectrum of integrable spin chains, revealing surprising patterns that challenge conventional wisdom about ground states and entanglement. The discovery that higher-spin systems can have their lowest-entropy states at maximum energy, rather than minimum energy, offers new insights into the relationship between quantum correlations and energy landscapes in exactly solvable models. These findings are particularly significant for quantum algorithm development, as understanding entanglement structure in integrable systems provides crucial benchmarks for quantum simulation algorithms and could inform strategies for preparing specific entangled states on quantum computers. The work's extension to non-compact spin chains and development of optimization algorithms for off-shell states opens new avenues for exploring quantum entanglement beyond traditional frameworks.

We investigate the quantum entanglement of Bethe states across a family of integrable spin chains, including the XXX$_{\frac{1}{2}}$ model, its higher-spin generalizations (XXX$_s$), and the non-compact $SL(2,\mathbb{R})$ chain. For on-shell eigenstates, we perform a comprehensive scan of the bipartite entanglement entropy across the entire spectrum of finite chains with periodic boundary conditions, and identify the Bethe solutions that minimize and maximize the entanglement. These extremal solutions follow systematic, spin-dependent patterns in the Bethe quantum numbers. In the XXX$_{\frac{1}{2}}$ spin chain, for the antiferromagnetic chain, the state with minimal entropy always coincides with the lowest-energy state (the ground state) within a given fixed-magnon sector. For the higher-spin XXX$_s$ model, however, the lowest-entropy state is not always identical to the ground state, and can even be the state of highest energy. By contrast, the Bethe roots that maximize entropy exhibit considerably more intricate structure. Our analysis further reveals how special Bethe root configurations, such as singular and strange solutions, affect entanglement, and it uncovers characteristic entanglement features in the non-compact $SL(2,\mathbb{R})$ chain that are absent from compact spin chains. For off-shell Bethe states, we develop an optimization algorithm that extremizes the entanglement entropy over rapidity distributions, enabling us to explore the maximum entanglement achievable by a Bethe state without imposing the Bethe ansatz equations.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IQM Radiance 54 Superconducting Quantum Computer Goes Live at CINECA in Italy
item.description:
Curator's Take: This marks a significant milestone for European quantum sovereignty, as IQM's 54-qubit superconducting system at CINECA represents one of the largest domestically-developed quantum computers now operational in Europe. The deployment at Italy's premier supercomputing facility creates an important hybrid classical-quantum research environment that could accelerate algorithm development and real-world applications across multiple scientific domains. IQM's growing presence in Europe, following successful deployments in Finland and Germany, demonstrates how regional quantum hardware companies are challenging the dominance of US tech giants in this critical technology sector. The integration with CINECA's high-performance computing infrastructure positions Italian researchers to explore complex problems that require both classical supercomputing power and quantum advantage.

Sovereign hardware developer IQM Quantum Computers, alongside the Italian Research Centre on High Performance Computing, Big Data, and Quantum Computing (ICSC), has announced the official inauguration of the IQM Radiance 54 quantum computer at the CINECA supercomputing facility. Located at the DAMA Tecnopolo in Bologna, the system represents a strategic technology asset engineered to accelerate [...] The post IQM Radiance 54 Superconducting Quantum Computer Goes Live at CINECA in Italy appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Pasqal Inaugurates Italy’s First Neutral-Atom Quantum Computer at CINECA Supercomputing Center
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone represents a significant step forward for Europe's quantum computing infrastructure, as Pasqal's SOL system brings cutting-edge neutral-atom quantum technology to Italy's premier research facilities for the first time. The deployment at CINECA's supercomputing center is particularly strategic, as it positions researchers to explore hybrid classical-quantum algorithms by integrating quantum processors with traditional high-performance computing resources. Neutral-atom platforms like Pasqal's offer unique advantages including flexible qubit connectivity and the ability to reconfigure atomic arrays dynamically, making this installation valuable for both fundamental research and practical algorithm development. The partnership with Italy's national research consortium signals growing European investment in quantum computing sovereignty and could accelerate quantum research across multiple disciplines within the region.

Neutral-atom quantum hardware developer Pasqal has officially inaugurated its SOL quantum computer at the DAMA Technopole in Emilia-Romagna, Bologna. Hosted by CINECA, Italy's largest public supercomputing consortium and a core member of the Italian Research Center on HPC, Big Data, and Quantum Computing (ICSC), the deployment marks the installation of Italy's first operational neutral-atom quantum [...] The post Pasqal Inaugurates Italy’s First Neutral-Atom Quantum Computer at CINECA Supercomputing Center appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: All about quantum error correction: distillation, mitigation, self-correction and beyond
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant theoretical breakthrough by revealing that seemingly disparate quantum error management techniques - from distillation to error mitigation to dynamical decoupling - are actually special cases of a single, more general quantum error correction framework. This unifying perspective could revolutionize how researchers approach quantum error handling, potentially leading to more efficient and systematic design of fault-tolerant quantum systems rather than treating each technique as an isolated solution. The work's extension to adaptive settings and multi-stage coding scenarios is particularly noteworthy, as it opens new pathways for developing quantum memories that can self-correct errors autonomously. For the quantum computing field racing toward practical fault tolerance, this theoretical foundation could accelerate the development of more robust quantum computers by providing a roadmap for combining and optimizing different error correction strategies.

In this work, it is shown that many quantum error-manipulating techniques, such as distillation, error mitigation, and dynamical decoupling, are special cases of the most general framework for quantum error correction. This unifying perspective is achieved by extending quantum error correction to include state-adaptive and channel-adaptive settings, as well as multi-stage coding scenarios. Based on this insight, a model of self-correcting quantum memory is also proposed. This work clarifies the relationship among these techniques and illustrates, through explicit constructions, how the unified perspective can guide the design of reliable quantum information systems.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Horizon Quantum Expands Hardware Testbed with 256-Qubit Trapped-Ion System Placement in Ireland
item.description:
Curator's Take: This expansion marks a significant step in Europe's quantum computing infrastructure development, as Horizon Quantum brings IonQ's advanced 256-qubit trapped-ion technology to Dublin through Ireland's ambitious Silicon Island strategy. The deployment represents more than just hardware placement - it signals Europe's growing commitment to establishing regional quantum capabilities rather than relying solely on systems housed in the US or other markets. With Ireland positioning itself as a semiconductor hub, this testbed could serve as a crucial access point for European researchers and companies looking to experiment with high-fidelity trapped-ion quantum computing. The partnership between Horizon's software infrastructure expertise and IonQ's proven hardware platform suggests a focus on making quantum computing more accessible to the broader European quantum ecosystem.

Integrated software infrastructure pioneer Horizon Quantum Holdings Ltd. has announced plans to deploy its second hardware testbed location at its European headquarters in Dublin, Ireland. The facility will host a sixth-generation, chip-based 256-qubit trapped-ion system developed by IonQ. Backed by Ireland’s National Semiconductor Strategy (Silicon Island) and supported by IDA Ireland, the hardware expansion builds [...] The post Horizon Quantum Expands Hardware Testbed with 256-Qubit Trapped-Ion System Placement in Ireland appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: High-fidelity entanglement and coherent multi-qubit mapping in an atom array
item.description:
Curator's Take: This Nature Physics study represents a significant step toward more versatile quantum computing architectures by demonstrating coherent transfer of entangled states between different types of qubits within the same ytterbium-171 atom system. The ability to map quantum information between different qubit encodings within atoms opens up powerful hybrid approaches where researchers can leverage the unique advantages of each qubit type - for example, using one encoding for long-term storage and another for fast gate operations. This coherent multi-qubit mapping capability could be particularly valuable for building more robust quantum processors that can dynamically optimize how they store and manipulate quantum information based on the specific computational task at hand. The work with ytterbium-171 atoms is especially noteworthy given this isotope's exceptional properties for quantum computing, including its long coherence times and precise control capabilities.

Nature Physics, Published online: 12 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03258-8 Ytterbium-171 atoms can store quantum information in different ways, with their own applications and advantages. Now the ability to coherently transfer entangled states between these different qubit types has been demonstrated.

Read original at Nature Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Logical qubits with erasure conversion using metastable neutral atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a significant step toward practical quantum error correction by demonstrating that metastable ytterbium-171 atoms can convert regular quantum errors into detectable "erasure" errors, which are much easier to correct than unknown errors. The ability to pinpoint exactly where errors occur, rather than having to guess their location, dramatically reduces the overhead required for quantum error correction protocols. This erasure conversion technique could substantially lower the threshold for achieving fault-tolerant quantum computing, bringing us closer to quantum computers that can run useful algorithms for extended periods without being overwhelmed by noise. The work builds on growing momentum in the field around erasure qubits, which many researchers now view as one of the most promising paths to scalable quantum computing.

Nature Physics, Published online: 12 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41567-026-03309-0 Qubits with detectable erasure errors can support more efficient error correction than qubits with unlocated errors. Improved logical qubit performance using erasure conversion has now been demonstrated using ytterbium-171 atoms.

Read original at Nature Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: An integrated ultrahigh vacuum cluster tool for diamond surface science and single nitrogen-vacancy center measurements
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article presents a significant advance in nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center research by creating an integrated system that can modify diamond surfaces and immediately measure how those changes affect individual NV centers without exposing the sample to air contamination. The ability to directly correlate surface chemistry with NV quantum properties addresses one of the biggest challenges in quantum sensing - understanding and controlling how surface conditions affect the performance of these atomic-scale quantum sensors. This integrated platform could accelerate the development of more reliable quantum sensors by enabling researchers to systematically engineer diamond surfaces that protect NV centers from decoherence while maintaining their sensitivity. The work represents an important step toward making NV-based quantum sensors more practical for real-world applications where surface stability is crucial.

We present a custom-designed ultrahigh vacuum (UHV) cluster tool developed for studying shallow nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, enabling in situ diamond surface preparation, characterization, and single NV center dynamics measurements within a single connected platform. The system combines a surface science chamber for controlled surface modification and analysis with a cryogenic confocal microscope chamber dedicated to NV spin and optical measurements. This integrated approach enables a direct correlation between diamond surface chemistry and the resulting NV spin and charge properties. The instrument provides a versatile platform for systematic studies of surface-induced decoherence mechanisms and charge dynamics for shallow NV centers, and establishes a pathway toward reproducible surface engineering for quantum sensing applications.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Simulation of Spin-Dependent Electron Transfer in a Synthetic Chiral Lattice with a Trapped Ion
item.description:
Curator's Take: This groundbreaking trapped ion experiment provides the first programmable quantum simulation of the chiral-induced spin selectivity effect, a mysterious phenomenon where electrons flowing through twisted molecular structures exhibit spin-dependent behavior that could revolutionize spintronics and quantum biology. The research directly demonstrates that quantum interference between different spin pathways drives this effect, settling a long-standing debate about the microscopic mechanisms behind chirality's influence on electron spin. What makes this particularly exciting is the unprecedented control the trapped ion platform offers - researchers can now tune system parameters with precision impossible in natural chiral molecules, opening new avenues for designing spin-selective materials and understanding quantum effects in biological systems like DNA and proteins.

Electron transfer through chiral structures can exhibit spin asymmetry, known as the chiral-induced spin selectivity effect, whose microscopic origin remains an open question. While path-interference within the chiral moiety has been proposed as a key mechanism, its experimental validation requires precise and versatile tunability of system parameters. Here we implement a programmable quantum simulation of spin-dependent electron transfer in a donor--chiral-bridge--acceptor model using a trapped ion. The bridge is encoded in internal states of the ion with tunable nearest- and next-nearest-neighbor couplings, while donor and acceptor states are coupled via a spectator bosonic motional mode. We observe spin-dependent interference within the bridge, and further reveal spin-dependence in donor-to-acceptor transfer dynamics, controlled by amplitude and phase of the coupling parameter. Our results identify interference among spin-dependent pathways as a microscopic origin of spin-dependent transfer, and open a route toward quantum simulations of complex chiral lattices with multi-level and bosonic degrees of freedom.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: BW Digital and NUS to Advance Quantum-Ready Data Centres for Southeast Asia
item.description:
Curator's Take: This collaboration represents a crucial step toward making quantum computing infrastructure viable in Southeast Asia's challenging tropical climate, where traditional data centers already struggle with heat and humidity management. The partnership between BW Digital and NUS addresses a often-overlooked but critical aspect of quantum deployment: the specialized cooling, power, and environmental controls that quantum computers require to operate reliably outside of laboratory settings. Southeast Asia's rapid digital transformation and strategic location make it a key battleground for quantum infrastructure development, and this research could establish engineering standards that enable practical quantum computing across one of the world's most economically dynamic regions. The focus on "quantum-ready" frameworks suggests they're preparing for the inevitable transition when quantum systems become commercially viable, positioning the region ahead of the curve rather than scrambling to catch up.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — BW Digital and the National University of Singapore’s College of Design and Engineering (NUS CDE) today announced a strategic research collaboration to develop the foundational engineering frameworks required for next-generation quantum-ready digital infrastructure in the tropical hubs of Southeast Asia. Announced at DCD>Connect | APAC 2026, Data Centre Dynamics’ flagship […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Generalized two-qubit Hamiltonian for Projective Quantum Feature Maps
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum computing's most pressing challenges: how to extract meaningful advantage from today's noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices by using them as sophisticated feature generators for classical machine learning. The team's generalized two-qubit Hamiltonian approach cleverly maximizes information density by encoding multiple classical variables along different Pauli axes of the same qubit, potentially squeezing more computational value from shallow quantum circuits that can survive current hardware limitations. What makes this particularly significant is their rigorous benchmarking across real IBM quantum processors with up to 156 qubits, showing consistent statistical improvements over classical baselines on biomedical datasets—though the authors appropriately note that performance remains highly dependent on specific implementation choices and hardware conditions. The release of their pqfmlib Python library could accelerate research in this promising direction toward demonstrating genuine near-term quantum advantage in machine learning applications.

Projected quantum feature maps provide a strategy for using quantum processors as feature generators for classical machine-learning models. Building on counterdiabatic Ising-glass and one-dimensional Heisenberg PQFMs, we introduce a generalized two-qubit Hamiltonian-based PQFM that provides a unified way to encode classical features through local Pauli fields and pairwise two-qubit Pauli interactions. This construction allows distinct classical variables to be embedded along different Pauli axes of the same qubit, increasing the information density of shallow circuits while remaining compatible with hardware constraints. We develop and implement these methods in pqfmlib, a publicly available Python library for constructing, executing, and benchmarking Hamiltonian-based PQFMs.We then benchmark the generalized Hamiltonian PQFMs against reference PQFMs on four biomedical classification datasets under a nested cross-validation protocol with paired statistical tests. Quantum features are generated using both IBM quantum processors with up to 156 qubits and statevector simulations. Our results show that the generalized two-qubit Hamiltonian family provides the most consistent pattern of statistically supported gains over matched classical baselines, although the performance of all methods depends on the dataset, encoding strategy, measured observables, and hardware conditions. These findings support generalized Hamiltonian PQFMs as a promising route toward near-term quantum utility.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Approximability limits for bounded-degree max-LINSAT and implications for decoded quantum interferometry
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article establishes fundamental theoretical limits for quantum optimization algorithms by proving that even sophisticated approaches like decoded quantum interferometry and QAOA cannot achieve more than constant-factor improvements over classical methods on bounded-degree constraint satisfaction problems. The work is particularly significant because it provides the first rigorous complexity-theoretic benchmarks for these quantum algorithms, showing that any quantum advantage must come from improved constant factors rather than asymptotic scaling improvements. This represents a sobering reality check for the quantum optimization community, as it mathematically constrains where quantum speedups can realistically be expected in practical optimization scenarios. The results also highlight an intriguing sensitivity to decoder choice in quantum interferometry methods, suggesting that hardware-software co-design may be crucial for extracting whatever quantum advantages remain possible within these theoretical bounds.

For general max-k-XORSAT with $k \geq 3$, no polynomial-time algorithm can do substantially better than random guessing on worst-case instances unless $\mathsf{P} = \mathsf{NP}$: approximating beyond the random-assignment value of $1/2$ is $\mathsf{NP}$-hard. The picture changes when each variable appears in at most $D$ constraints. In that bounded-degree setting, polynomial-time algorithms can provably beat the random baseline by an additive amount of order $1/\sqrt{D}$. For Boolean instances, this scaling is known to be optimal: the matching hardness result is due to Trevisan, while the corresponding algorithmic guarantee was established by Barak et al. Whether the same holds over general finite fields, and what it implies for quantum algorithms, has not been established. We make this connection explicit and extend the hardness to max-E$k$-LINSAT$(q,r)$ with bounded degree $D$ and over arbitrary finite fields $\mathbb{F}_q$, proving that it is $\mathsf{NP}$-hard to exceed $r/q + \mathcal{O}_{q,r}(1/\sqrt{D})$. These results provide the complexity-theoretic benchmark for the bounded-degree instances targeted by decoded quantum interferometry (DQI), QAOA, and classical heuristics. Any quantum advantage on bounded-degree instances is therefore confined to the constant prefactor. We further show that in the context of DQI and on $(k,D)$-regular instances, this prefactor is sensitive to the nature of the decoder: DQI with classical decoders faces an information-theoretic $1/\sqrt{D \log D}$ barrier that prevents it from matching the hardness scaling, while DQI with quantum decoders is compatible with the $1/\sqrt{D}$ scaling -- identifying quantum decoding as the key ingredient for matching the complexity-theoretic scaling with DQI.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: A ribbon ZX calculus for gauge theory
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article represents a fascinating theoretical bridge between quantum computing's graphical languages and fundamental physics, extending the popular ZX calculus framework to handle gauge theories that underpin our understanding of forces in nature. While ZX calculus has become a powerful tool for designing and optimizing quantum circuits, this work opens the door to using these same graphical methods to tackle problems in quantum field theory and potentially low-dimensional gravity. The connection through Hopf Frobenius algebras suggests that quantum computing's abstract mathematical tools might offer fresh computational approaches to some of physics' most challenging theoretical problems. Though highly theoretical at present, this type of cross-pollination between quantum information and fundamental physics has historically led to unexpected practical applications in both fields.

ZX calculus provides a graphical formalism for reasoning about quantum processes, built from two interacting Frobenius algebras associated with the Z and X bases of a qubit. While it has found widespread application in quantum information and computing, its relationship to quantum field theory has only recently begun to be explored. In this work, we further develop this connection by providing a generalization of ZX calculus to two-dimensional Yang Mills theory with a compact gauge group. The key observation is that both frameworks can be organized around the Hopf Frobenius algebraic structure associated with a group algebra, which can in turn be described by the diagrammatics of two dimensional topological quantum field theory. Given the well known relationship between gauge theory and gravity in two and three dimensions, our work paves the way for applications of ZX to low dimensional gravity.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Generalized Exact Fractional Quantum Information Model with Memory Effects
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research extends quantum information theory into the realm of fractional calculus, where traditional derivatives are replaced with non-integer order operations that can capture memory effects and nonlocal behavior in quantum systems. While highly theoretical, this work could prove significant for modeling quantum systems where standard approaches fall short, such as certain condensed matter systems or quantum systems with complex environmental interactions that exhibit long-range correlations. The authors demonstrate how fundamental information measures like Shannon entropy and Fisher information behave differently when fractional derivatives are involved, potentially opening new pathways for understanding information flow in quantum systems with memory. Though still in early stages, this mathematical framework could eventually inform the design of quantum algorithms or error correction schemes that account for non-Markovian effects in real quantum hardware.

In this paper, we analyze quantum information measures in fractional quantum mechanics using the Riemann-Liouville derivative formalism adopted here. In this case, we initially reconsider the conventional definitions of Shannon entropy and Fisher information, subsequently extending them to fractional quantum systems described by nonlocal differential operator frameworks adopted. Within this generalized formulation, fractional expressions of Shannon entropy and Fisher information are constructed and their mathematical structures examined thoroughly. Also, the formalism is then applied to the quantum harmonic oscillator, yielding explicit analytical expressions derived as functions of the fractional parameter therein. The obtained results demonstrate that fractional derivatives alter the localization properties of probability densities and generate nontrivial variations in information content and sensitivity across system behavior. In this context, the fractional parameter plays a central role in controlling deviations from the standard quantum information measures framework. Also, the study establishes a consistent framework for describing information-theoretic properties of quantum systems governed by nonlocal dynamics.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Observation of Non-Gaussian Magnon Dynamics in a Two-Dimensional Long-Range XY Model
item.description:
Curator's Take: This work represents a significant step toward demonstrating quantum advantage in many-body physics simulations using trapped ion systems. The researchers successfully observed non-Gaussian magnon dynamics - a quantum phenomenon that classical computers struggle to simulate efficiently - by carefully engineering a two-dimensional XY model with long-range interactions. What makes this particularly compelling is their robust experimental approach that accounts for real-world challenges like decoherence and calibration errors, showing they can reliably distinguish genuine quantum behavior from classical effects. This provides a concrete pathway for quantum simulators to tackle problems beyond the reach of classical computation, moving closer to practical quantum advantage in understanding complex magnetic materials and spin systems.
