Non-Gaussian evolution of high-order spin correlations characterizes important properties of quantum many-body systems. In practice, decoherence, statistical fluctuation and miscalibration of experimental parameters all hinder the witness of non-Gaussian dynamics. Here we demonstrate the crossover between Gaussian and non-Gaussian dynamics on a two-dimensional XY model with long-range and spatially structured interaction using a trapped ion quantum simulator. We prepare different initial densities of magnon excitations and verify the dynamics of single-spin observables for the engineered Hamiltonian. Then we compare the high-order spin correlations with the mean-field solution and the Holstein-Primakoff approximation, and demonstrate the non-Gaussian behavior in a way independent of the calibration errors. Our work provides a verifiable path from classically simulatable dynamics to regimes where quantum advantage may emerge.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Nu Quantum Demonstrates Subsystem Erasure Tolerance in Networked QPU Architectures
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research from Nu Quantum tackles one of the most pressing challenges in scaling quantum computing: what happens when entire quantum processing units fail catastrophically rather than just experiencing typical qubit errors. Traditional quantum error correction focuses on individual qubit failures, but Nu Quantum's distributed framework demonstrates how networked quantum systems can continue operating even when complete subsystems go offline. This "subsystem erasure tolerance" represents a crucial step toward building resilient, large-scale quantum computing infrastructure where individual QPU failures don't bring down the entire system. The work provides both theoretical foundations and practical simulations for fault-tolerant quantum networks, which will be essential as we move toward distributed quantum computing architectures connecting multiple quantum processors.

Distributed quantum hardware developer Nu Quantum Ltd. has reported structural mechanics and numerical simulations validating a fault-tolerant network framework capable of tolerating the complete failure of individual Quantum Processing Units (QPUs). Detailed in a technical manuscript deposited on the open-access arXiv repository, the research introduces a distributed quantum error correction (QEC) paradigm that handles catastrophic [...] The post Nu Quantum Demonstrates Subsystem Erasure Tolerance in Networked QPU Architectures appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Alice & Bob Launches On-Premise “Helium Quantum System” for Cat-Qubit Research
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article marks a significant milestone for Alice & Bob as they evolve from a quantum chip manufacturer into a full-stack quantum computing company, joining the ranks of players like IBM and Rigetti who offer complete quantum systems. The Helium system's focus on cat-qubits represents a fascinating alternative approach to quantum error correction, as these "noise-biased" qubits are theoretically more resistant to certain types of errors that plague conventional superconducting qubits. By targeting their first logical qubit implementation with 18 physical cat-qubits, Alice & Bob is positioning itself at the forefront of the race toward practical fault-tolerant quantum computing, where logical qubits protected by quantum error correction will be essential for running useful algorithms. The move to on-premise systems also signals growing maturity in the quantum industry, as companies recognize that researchers need hands-on access to experiment with novel qubit architectures rather than relying solely on cloud access.

Fault-tolerant hardware developer Alice & Bob has unveiled the Helium Quantum System, transitioning the company from chip-level manufacturing into delivering full-stack, on-premise quantum computing platforms. Operating on a noise-biased cat-qubit architecture, the integrated machine is engineered to encode the company's first logical qubit using an array of 18 physical cat-qubits. The platform is intentionally opened [...] The post Alice & Bob Launches On-Premise “Helium Quantum System” for Cat-Qubit Research appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Reduced basis algorithm for solving nonlinear differential equations on quantum computers
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most fundamental limitations for scientific applications: how to simulate nonlinear systems when quantum mechanics itself is inherently linear. The researchers' reduced basis algorithm cleverly sidesteps this challenge by preprocessing nonlinear differential equations into linear operators that can run natively on quantum hardware, maintaining exact accuracy while keeping qubit requirements logarithmic in grid size rather than exponential. This breakthrough could unlock quantum advantages for critical scientific computing problems like fluid dynamics, climate modeling, and materials science, where nonlinear PDEs are ubiquitous but currently intractable on quantum computers. The approach represents a significant step toward making quantum computers genuinely useful for real-world scientific simulations beyond the toy problems typically demonstrated today.

As quantum computing moves toward scientific computing applications, nonlinear differential equations remain a central challenge since quantum evolution is intrinsically linear. In this work, we introduce a reduced basis algorithm (RBA) for polynomial nonlinear ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and spatially discretized partial differential equations (PDEs). After time discretization, the method composes the resulting polynomial update map over $m$ timesteps, identifies the reduced monomial basis appearing in this composed map, and constructs a linear RBA operator whose action recovers the exact $m$-timestep nonlinear dynamics. Thus, at the level of the chosen discrete update rule, the method introduces no additional approximation error beyond the time discretization error. The qubit number requirement is governed by the size of the reduced monomial basis. For an $n$-dimensional polynomial ODE system of degree $p>1$, the lifted register requires at most $q_m^{\mathrm{ODE}} = O(nm\log p)$ qubits in the full basis scenario. For PDEs discretized on $N^D$ grid points, a locality-based construction requires at most $q_m^{\mathrm{PDE}} = O(D\log N + n m^{D+1}\log p)$ qubits. Hence, the dependence on the grid size remains logarithmic, while the nonlinear overhead is controlled by local reduced basis size. The main computational burden is moved from the quantum computer to a classical preprocessing step, where the reduced monomial basis and RBA operator are constructed for the chosen timestep window. Through numerical tests on the Lorenz system and the one-dimensional Burgers equation, we verify that the RBA reproduces the corresponding discrete time nonlinear dynamics exactly, while exposing the trade-off between timestep composition, reduced basis growth, and locality.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Australia’s NRFC and Firgun Ventures Inject 40 Million AUD ($28 Million USD) and Growth Capital into Silicon Quantum Computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This funding announcement signals Australia's serious commitment to establishing itself as a major player in the global quantum computing race, with the government-backed National Reconstruction Fund Corporation doubling down on Silicon Quantum Computing's silicon-based qubit approach. What makes this particularly noteworthy is SQC's focus on silicon qubits, which could potentially leverage existing semiconductor manufacturing infrastructure and offer better scalability than some competing technologies. The combination of sovereign funding and international venture capital from Firgun Ventures suggests growing confidence in Australia's quantum ecosystem and SQC's technical progress toward practical quantum processors. This investment underscores how nations are increasingly viewing quantum computing as a strategic technology requiring substantial public-private partnerships to compete on the world stage.

Australian silicon qubit developer Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC) has secured a dual capital injection anchored by a 40 million AUD ($28 million USD) follow-on commitment from the sovereign National Reconstruction Fund Corporation (NRFC) alongside a strategic growth investment from London-based venture capital firm Firgun Ventures. The newly allocated funding builds directly upon the NRFC's initial [...] The post Australia’s NRFC and Firgun Ventures Inject 40 Million AUD ($28 Million USD) and Growth Capital into Silicon Quantum Computing appeared first on Quantum Computing Report .

Read original at Quantum Computing Report
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Pasqal Launches Italy’s First Neutral-Atom Quantum Computer at CINECA
item.description:
Curator's Take: This marks a significant milestone for European quantum infrastructure as Italy joins the growing network of nations deploying neutral-atom quantum computers for research and development. Pasqal's expansion into Italy through CINECA represents more than just geographic diversification - it demonstrates how quantum computing is becoming integrated into existing high-performance computing ecosystems, where quantum systems can eventually work alongside classical supercomputers. The partnership with Italy's largest public supercomputing operator signals that European research institutions are moving beyond experimental quantum projects toward building practical quantum-classical hybrid computing capabilities. This deployment also strengthens Europe's position in the global quantum race, particularly in neutral-atom technology where Pasqal competes directly with American companies like QuEra and Atom Computing.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Pasqal, one of the global leaders in neutral-atom quantum computing, today announced the inauguration of Europe’s third Pasqal quantum computer hosted at CINECA, Italy’s largest public supercomputing operator and a member of the Italian Research Center on High Performance Computing (ICSC), Big Data, and Quantum Computing, in Bologna, Italy. The […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Foundations of Practical Quantum Advantage in Quantum-Informed Machine Learning for Predicting Chaos
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research presents a compelling theoretical framework for achieving genuine quantum advantage in machine learning applications for chaotic systems like weather prediction and turbulence modeling. The key breakthrough lies in proving that quantum systems can extract complex spatial correlations from chaotic data using just two quantum copies, while classical methods would require exponentially more measurements - a mathematically rigorous quantum-classical separation that goes beyond typical quantum machine learning claims. The practical validation is particularly noteworthy, demonstrating 10-39% improvements in weather forecasting accuracy across multiple time horizons using real European weather data, suggesting this approach could meaningfully impact climate modeling and prediction systems. This work stands out in the quantum machine learning landscape by providing both solid theoretical foundations and concrete performance gains on real-world chaotic systems, addressing long-standing questions about where quantum computing can deliver practical advantages.

We develop theoretical foundations for a practical quantum-advantage mechanism in quantum-informed machine learning for chaotic dynamical systems. A family of k-indexed higher-order quantum statistical priors (Q-Priors) hosts the k-point marginal of the invariant measure on n_q = kq qubits, extending the single-site construction of prior work. We prove a two-stage advantage. In the representation stage, superposition and entanglement compactly store non-factorisable spatial correlations of the invariant measure on n_q qubits. In the extraction stage, joint Bell measurements on two copies estimate any post hoc Pauli functional with a copy-pair count independent of n_q, whereas any adaptive single-copy protocol for the corresponding full-Pauli read-out requires Omega(2^(n_q)) copies; this is a provable quantum-classical separation in copy-measurement complexity. The two-copy read-out is realised in simulation and on IQM superconducting processors. Two case studies instantiate the mechanism in workflows of independent scientific value: a turbulent channel-flow study in which the two-copy read-out yields a named non-diagonal correlator of the invariant measure (the velocity-direction coherence), and a medium-range weather forecasting workflow on the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts ERA5 reanalysis in which the diagonal k <= 2 Q-Prior steers a Koopman rollout, improves anomaly-correlation skill by 10-39% across 48-240 h lead times, and reduces the long-horizon collapse of rollouts onto a static mean field. The two conditions of our practical-advantage definition are met at complementary levels, identifying a candidate route to practical quantum advantage before fault-tolerant hardware.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Horizon Quantum Selects Dublin for Second Quantum Computer Testbed
item.description:
Curator's Take: This development signals Horizon Quantum's ambitious push to establish Europe as a major hub for quantum computing infrastructure, with Dublin emerging as a strategic location that could rival established quantum centers in the US and Asia. The deployment of an IonQ 256-qubit trapped ion system represents a significant leap in accessible quantum computing power for European researchers and enterprises, potentially accelerating quantum software development and applications across the continent. What's particularly noteworthy is Horizon Quantum's focus on software infrastructure rather than hardware development, positioning them to bridge the critical gap between quantum hardware capabilities and practical applications that businesses can actually use. This move could catalyze a new wave of quantum innovation in Europe by providing local access to cutting-edge quantum systems without the complexities of international data transfer restrictions.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — Horizon Quantum Holdings Ltd. (“Horizon Quantum”), a pioneer of software infrastructure for quantum applications, today announced that it expects to locate its second quantum computer—anticipated to be one of the most advanced commercial quantum systems in the world—in Dublin, Ireland. By placing this IonQ 256-qubit system at its European headquarters, […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IQM Quantum Computer Installed at CINECA Supercomputing Center in Italy
item.description:
Curator's Take: This installation marks a significant step in Europe's quantum computing infrastructure development, positioning CINECA as a key hub for quantum-classical hybrid computing research. The IQM Radiance 54's integration with one of Europe's premier supercomputing centers creates exciting opportunities for researchers to explore quantum advantages in optimization and machine learning applications that require substantial classical computational support. What makes this particularly noteworthy is the strategic placement at CINECA, which serves researchers across multiple European countries and could accelerate collaborative quantum research projects. The timing also coincides with Europe's broader push for quantum sovereignty, reducing dependence on non-European quantum computing platforms while building domestic expertise in this critical technology sector.

Insider Brief PRESS RELEASE — The Italian Research Centre on High Performance Computing, Big Data, and Quantum Computing (ICSC) today inaugurated the IQM Radiance 54 quantum computer at CINECA, one of Europe’s leading supercomputing centers, enabling advanced applications in optimization, simulation, and machine learning. IQM has previously announced its ongoing business combination with Real Asset Acquisition Corp (Nasdaq: RAAQ), […]

Read original at The Quantum Insider
item.author: Mark Eatherly
